title: PSLE Science — Water Cycle and Matter

questions.clue: This is the continuous journey that water takes as it moves between the Earth's surface and the sky above. It involves water changing form and moving through the environment over and over again without ever stopping.
questions.funFact: The same water has been going through this process for over 4 billion years — the water in your glass today may have once been drunk by a dinosaur!

questions.clue: This happens when liquid water on a surface gains heat and slowly changes into an invisible gas. It happens at any temperature and only occurs at the surface of the liquid. It is faster when it is hotter or windier or when there is a larger surface area.
questions.funFact: Your wet footprints on the ground after swimming disappear because of this process — the water gains heat from the surroundings and rises into the air as invisible gas!

questions.clue: This phrase describes exactly what liquid water does when it absorbs energy from its surroundings — it slowly changes into an invisible gas at its surface. This is why puddles on the ground disappear even without being wiped away.
questions.funFact: The ocean loses an enormous amount of water this way every single day — scientists estimate about 1 million billion litres leaves the ocean surface through this process every year!

questions.clue: This happens when invisible water in the air loses heat and turns back into tiny visible water droplets on a cool surface or high up in the sky. This is how water appears on the outside of a cold drink can and how clouds are formed.
questions.funFact: The water drops on the outside of a cold drink can did not come from inside the can — they formed from invisible water already floating in the air around it that lost heat and became visible!

questions.clue: This phrase describes what invisible water in the air does when it meets a cold surface or rises high into the cooler sky — it gives up energy to the surroundings and changes back into tiny visible droplets that we can see.
questions.funFact: When you breathe out on a cold morning you can see your breath — the invisible water in your warm breath loses heat in the cold air and changes into tiny visible droplets right in front of you!

questions.clue: This is when tiny water droplets in clouds join together to form larger and larger drops that become too heavy to stay floating in the sky and fall back down to the Earth's surface. In Singapore this almost always happens in one form.
questions.funFact: The largest recorded drops of this that ever fell measured nearly 1 centimetre wide — about the size of a small marble — recorded over Brazil and the Marshall Islands!

questions.clue: This is the final stage of the water journey where water that has fallen from the sky gathers together in rivers, lakes and oceans on the Earth's surface before the whole cycle starts over again.
questions.funFact: Singapore channels rainwater into large man-made lakes so efficiently that it can collect and use water on a scale that very few countries in the world can match for its small land size!

questions.clue: This is the invisible gas form of water that is always floating in the air all around us. We cannot see it at all but when it loses heat and meets a cool surface it turns back into tiny visible droplets.
questions.funFact: The air in a tropical country like Singapore contains so much of this that you can feel the air is heavy and sticky — scientists call this having high humidity!

questions.clue: These form when invisible water in the air loses heat and condenses. Millions of these floating together in the sky make up a cloud. They are also what makes your bathroom mirror go foggy after a hot shower.
questions.funFact: A single cloud can contain millions of these but each one is so incredibly light that it floats — it takes billions of them clumping together to become heavy enough to fall as rain!

questions.clue: This forms when invisible water in the air near the ground loses heat and condenses into tiny droplets very close to the Earth's surface. It makes the surrounding air look hazy and blurry.
questions.funFact: In Singapore you sometimes see this at Bukit Timah Hill very early in the morning — the cool forest air causes the invisible water nearby to condense into tiny floating droplets close to the ground!

questions.clue: These form high up in the sky when water vapour rises, loses heat and condenses into millions of tiny water droplets that float together as a large visible white mass in the sky.
questions.funFact: Despite looking soft and fluffy, a typical one of these weighs about the same as 100 elephants — but the droplets are spread over such a huge volume that the entire mass still floats!

questions.clue: This is a large man-made lake built by people to store fresh water that has been collected from rainfall and rivers so that it can be used for drinking and daily needs.
questions.funFact: Singapore has 17 of these covering two thirds of the island's land area — making it one of the very few countries in the world that collects and stores its own rainwater on such an impressive scale!

questions.clue: This is one of the three states of matter. It has a definite shape and a definite volume that it keeps on its own without needing any container. It cannot flow, cannot be poured and cannot be squashed into a smaller space.
questions.funFact: Even though ice looks and feels like a typical example of this state, it actually takes up more space than the liquid form of the same substance — which is why ice cubes float instead of sinking in your drink!

questions.clue: This is one of the three states of matter. It has a definite volume but no fixed shape — it flows and takes on the shape of whatever container it is poured into. It cannot be squashed into a smaller space.
questions.funFact: Honey is in this state but flows so incredibly slowly that it can take years to drain fully from a container — it is one of the most sluggish natural substances found on Earth!

questions.clue: This is one of the three states of matter. It has no fixed shape and no fixed volume — it spreads out to completely fill whatever container it is placed in. Unlike solids and liquids, it can be squashed into a smaller space.
questions.funFact: The air all around you right now is a mixture of several types of this state of matter — mostly nitrogen at 78 percent and oxygen at 21 percent with very small amounts of other types!

questions.clue: This is a property of solids only. It means the object always keeps its own form no matter what container you place it in or where you put it. A wooden block looks exactly the same whether it is in a box, on a table or on the floor.
questions.funFact: A rubber eraser has this property — even though rubber is flexible and can be squeezed, when you let go it returns to the same form it started with, unlike water which always takes a new shape!

questions.clue: This is a property shared by solids and liquids — it means the substance always takes up the same fixed amount of space no matter what container it is placed in. Gases do not have this property because they expand to fill any space.
questions.funFact: 100 millilitres of water always takes up exactly 100 millilitres of space whether it is in a tall thin tube or a wide flat bowl — this is what it means to have this property!

questions.clue: This is a property of liquids and gases. It means the substance has no fixed form of its own and will always take on the shape of whatever container it is placed inside. The same liquid looks different in every differently shaped container.
questions.funFact: Pour orange juice into a tall thin glass and it looks tall and thin — pour the same juice into a wide flat bowl and it spreads out wide and flat — this is because liquids have this property!

questions.clue: This describes a substance that can be squashed into a smaller space when a force is applied to it. Only one of the three states of matter has this property. The other two states cannot be squashed into a smaller space no matter how much force is used.
questions.funFact: This property of air is exactly what makes bicycle tyres, footballs and basketballs work — air is forced into a small enclosed space and its outward push makes the object firm and bouncy!

questions.clue: This describes a property of solids and liquids — they cannot be squashed into a smaller space no matter how much force is applied to them. This is why hydraulic machines that use liquid inside can transmit force so effectively.
questions.funFact: Car brakes work because brake fluid has this property — when you press the brake pedal the force is transmitted perfectly through the liquid to the brake pads because the liquid cannot be squashed at all!

questions.clue: This is the change of state where a substance gains heat and changes from its firm fixed form into a flowing pourable form. It happens at a fixed and specific temperature that is different for every pure substance.
questions.funFact: Chocolate goes through this process in your mouth — your body temperature of 37 degrees Celsius is just high enough to cause it, which is why good quality chocolate feels so wonderfully smooth!

questions.clue: This is the change of state where a flowing pourable substance loses heat and becomes firm with a fixed shape. It is the opposite of melting and happens at exactly the same specific temperature as melting but going the other way.
questions.funFact: Water expands when it goes through this process — which is why water pipes burst in very cold countries when the water inside changes state and pushes outward hard against the walls of the pipe!

questions.clue: This is when a liquid gains a large amount of heat and rapidly changes into a gas throughout its entire volume all at the same time. Bubbles of gas form throughout the liquid and rise up to the surface. It only happens at one fixed temperature.
questions.funFact: Water does this at a lower temperature on top of a high mountain because there is less air pressing down on it — on Mount Everest this happens at only 70 degrees Celsius instead of the usual 100!

questions.clue: These are two ways a liquid can change into a gas but they are different in important ways. One happens slowly at the surface only at any temperature while the other happens rapidly throughout the entire liquid but only at one fixed temperature.
questions.funFact: You can tell which one is happening by looking — if you see bubbles forming throughout the liquid and rising up it is the rapid one happening at the fixed temperature. If the liquid just quietly disappears over time it is the slow surface one!

questions.clue: This is the exact temperature at which a solid gains enough heat to begin changing into a liquid. Every pure substance has its own specific fixed value for this and it can be used to help identify what a substance is.
questions.funFact: The value of this for pure ice is exactly 0 degrees Celsius — scientists use this as one of the two fixed reference points on the Celsius scale that the entire world uses to measure temperature today!

questions.clue: This is the exact temperature at which a liquid rapidly changes into a gas throughout its entire volume with bubbles forming everywhere. For water this is exactly 100 degrees Celsius under normal everyday conditions.
questions.funFact: Different liquids have very different values for this — alcohol reaches its value at only 78 degrees Celsius, which is why hand sanitiser disappears from your hands so much faster than plain water does!

questions.clue: This is the exact temperature at which a liquid loses enough heat to change completely into a solid. For water this is 0 degrees Celsius — the same number as its melting point but the change happens in the opposite direction.
questions.funFact: Salt lowers this value for water — which is exactly why countries spread salt on icy roads in winter to stop the water on the road surface from remaining in its solid slippery state!

questions.clue: This is when a substance changes from one physical form to another because it either gains or loses heat. The substance itself stays exactly the same type of material — only the form it exists in is different.
questions.funFact: Butter goes through one of these when you cook with it — and it can go back to its original form when cooled because the butter has not changed into any different substance at all!

questions.clue: This is the phrase used in science to describe normal everyday indoor conditions without any heating or cooling. In Singapore this is usually around 25 to 30 degrees Celsius. Scientists use this term to say what state a substance is normally found in.
questions.funFact: Mercury is the only metal that exists as a liquid at this condition — every other metal on Earth is completely solid under normal everyday conditions, which makes mercury extremely unusual!

questions.clue: This is the amount of matter in an object measured in grams or kilograms. It does not change when a substance changes state — the same amount of this is present whether the substance is a solid, a liquid or a gas.
questions.funFact: When ice melts into water and then evaporates into vapour the total amount of this stays exactly the same throughout every single change of state — matter is never created or destroyed!

questions.clue: This is an important science principle that states the total amount of matter in a substance stays exactly the same before and after a change of state. Whether it is solid, liquid or gas the total amount of matter does not change.
questions.funFact: If you weigh a sealed container of ice and then melt all the ice into water and weigh it again — the reading on the scale will be exactly identical because this principle always holds true!

questions.clue: This is the amount of space that a substance takes up. It is measured in millilitres or litres for liquids. Unlike mass, this value can change when a substance changes state — for example water takes up more space when it becomes ice.
questions.funFact: Water takes up about 9 percent MORE space when it freezes into ice — most other substances shrink when they solidify but water does the surprising opposite and expands!

questions.clue: These are the three different physical forms that all substances can exist in depending on how much heat energy they contain. Every substance on Earth can exist in all three of these forms under the right temperature conditions.
questions.funFact: Water is the perfect example to understand all three of these because we see all of them in everyday life — as ice, as the liquid we drink and as the invisible vapour in the air around us every single day!

questions.clue: This is the process of adding heat energy to a substance. When enough of this is done to a solid it melts into a liquid. When enough is done to a liquid it boils into a gas. It causes substances to change from one state to the next.
questions.funFact: The Sun does this to the surface of Earth's oceans every single day — converting enormous amounts of liquid water into invisible gas that rises into the atmosphere and eventually forms clouds!

questions.clue: This is the process of removing heat energy from a substance. When enough of this is done to a gas it condenses back into a liquid. When enough is done to a liquid it freezes into a solid. It causes substances to change to a lower state.
questions.funFact: Your refrigerator does this constantly to the air inside it — keeping the temperature low enough to slow down the growth of bacteria and keep your food fresh for much longer!
